---
title: "Day 3 — OpenMP synchronisation, memory model, tasks"
subtitle: "Morning lecture → afternoon A2 lab"
author: "Gerard Gorman"
institute: "Department of Earth Science and Engineering — Imperial College London"
date: today
date-format: long
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    slide-number: c/t
    incremental: false
    code-overflow: wrap
    code-block-height: 600px
    highlight-style: github
    smaller: true
    controls: true
---

## Day 3 roadmap

**Morning** (09:00 – 12:00) — All lecturing, with short exercises.

1. A1 retrospective
2. Synchronisation primitives (barriers / critical / atomic / locks)
3. The OpenMP-5.1 memory model
4. `single` / `masked` / `nowait`
5. Tasks, task dependences, `taskloop`
6. A2 lab brief

**Afternoon** (14:00 – 17:00) — A2 lab. No new material; instructor on hand.

End-of-day deliverable: A2 complete (both variants + CHOICE.md + measurement table).

## A1 retrospective {visibility="uncounted"}

Yesterday you parallelised an integration kernel with non-uniform per-iter cost.

- **What did the schedule sweep show?** Show of hands: who picked `static`? `dynamic, 64`? `guided`?
- **What were your speedup curves at 16 / 64 / 128?** Where did they bend away from linear?
- **What single thing surprised you?** Common reports: `static` was fine for small N; `dynamic` won for large N because of the spike region.

Your `tables.csv` from yesterday should make this conversation evidence-based. If yours doesn't, fix that today.

## Why synchronisation? {visibility="uncounted"}

Threads need to agree on what they've done.

- **Read-after-write** — thread A writes `x`; thread B reads `x`; without synchronisation B might read the old value.
- **Mutual exclusion** — only one thread should update a shared counter at a time.
- **Phase boundaries** — all threads should finish "produce" before any starts "consume".

OpenMP's tools: barriers, critical sections, atomic ops, locks, the memory model (acquire/release), and reductions (covered yesterday).

## Barriers — implicit and explicit

```cpp
#pragma omp parallel
{
    do_phase_a();
#pragma omp barrier         // explicit — every thread waits here
    do_phase_b();
}                           // implicit barrier at region exit
```

- Every thread must reach the barrier before any thread proceeds.
- **Implicit barriers** at the end of: `parallel`, `for`, `single`, `sections`.
- Use `nowait` to strip an implicit barrier when the next stage has no dependence (covered later).

## Implicit barriers under `for`

```cpp
#pragma omp parallel default(none) shared(a, b, n)
{
#pragma omp for                  // implicit barrier at end
    for (size_t i = 0; i < n; ++i) a[i] = init(i);
    // ← every thread sees a[i] consistent before continuing

#pragma omp for
    for (size_t i = 0; i < n; ++i) b[i] = a[i] * 2;
}
```

The barrier between the two `for` constructs is what makes `b[i] = a[i] * 2` correct — without it, thread B might read `a[i]` while thread A is still writing it.

## `nowait` — lifting the implicit barrier

```cpp
#pragma omp for nowait
for (...) { unrelated_work_a(); }
// no barrier — fast threads continue immediately

#pragma omp for
for (...) { unrelated_work_b(); }
```

- Strips the implicit barrier from one worksharing construct.
- **Only safe when the next stage has no dependence on the previous one.**
- A favourite source of subtle bugs — when in doubt, leave the barrier in.

## The orphan-construct rule

A worksharing directive (`for`, `single`, `sections`) is *orphaned* if it appears outside any enclosing `parallel` region — typically inside a function:

```cpp
void worker(std::vector<double>& v) {
#pragma omp for default(none) shared(v)        // orphaned for
    for (size_t i = 0; i < v.size(); ++i) v[i] *= 2.0;
}

#pragma omp parallel
{ worker(v); }   // orphan binds to *this* enclosing parallel region
```

- Orphan binds to the dynamically enclosing `parallel` region.
- Outside any `parallel`, `worker` runs serially — no error, just no parallelism.
- See `assessment/handouts/orphaned-reduction.md` for a deep dive on orphan-reduction edge cases.

## `critical` — semantics

```cpp
#pragma omp critical
{
    // arbitrary code; only one thread inside at a time, globally.
}
```

- General-purpose mutex. Any code can go inside.
- Expensive — every thread serialises through one global lock.
- Use only when atomic / reduction / lock can't express what you need.

## `critical` — naming and granularity

```cpp
#pragma omp critical (bucket_a)        // one mutex per name
{ update_bucket_a(); }

#pragma omp critical (bucket_b)        // independent mutex
{ update_bucket_b(); }
```

- A *named* critical lets two regions with different names run in parallel.
- Granularity is tied to *source code*, not data — if you have 1024 buckets, you can't have 1024 critical names.
- For data-driven granularity (one mutex per data structure instance), use explicit locks.

## `atomic` — semantics + supported ops

```cpp
#pragma omp atomic
counter += 1;             // single read-modify-write
```

Compiles to a CPU atomic instruction (`lock xadd` on x86-64, etc.). Much cheaper than `critical`.

Supported single-op forms (5.1):

- `x op= expr` — `+=`, `-=`, `*=`, `/=`, bit-ops, etc.
- `x++`, `++x`, `x--`, `--x`
- `x = x op expr`

Anything more complex (multi-statement update, branching) needs `critical`.

## `atomic` — `read` / `write` / `update` / `capture`

5.1 explicitly names four atomic *clauses*:

```cpp
#pragma omp atomic read
v = shared_x;

#pragma omp atomic write
shared_x = v;

#pragma omp atomic update     // default if no clause given
shared_x += v;

#pragma omp atomic capture    // read-modify-write + capture old value
{ tmp = shared_x; shared_x += 1; }
```

Plus a memory-ordering clause: `seq_cst` (sequential consistency — the strongest, default), `acquire`, `release`, `acq_rel` (acquire-and-release), `relaxed`. We'll use `acquire`/`release` shortly — the slides on memory visibility define what they mean.

## Choosing: `critical` vs `atomic` vs `reduction` vs `lock`

| Need | Use |
|---|---|
| Many independent updates to one shared counter | `reduction` |
| A single arithmetic update, occasionally | `atomic update` |
| A small block of code under one global lock | `critical` |
| Many independent locks, one per data instance | `omp_lock_t` |
| Read-modify-write of a complex data structure | `critical` (or finer locks) |

Default to `reduction`; reach for the next tool only when you must.

## Same answer, three ways — `critical`

{{< include _partials/day3/critical_vs_atomic__critical.qmd >}}

- General-purpose mutual exclusion: any thread holding the (anonymous) lock blocks the rest.
- Arbitrary loop bodies inside the `critical` block are allowed — that flexibility is the price you pay for the lock cost.
- Slowest of the three at high thread counts.

## Same answer, three ways — `atomic`

{{< include _partials/day3/critical_vs_atomic__atomic.qmd >}}

- A single hardware atomic read-modify-write — faster than `critical` because there's no lock acquire/release.
- Restricted to one supported RMW expression on a single scalar (`+=`, `-=`, `*=`, `&=`, `|=`, `^=`, `++`, `--`, etc.).
- Faster than `critical`, slower than `reduction`.

## Same answer, three ways — `reduction`

{{< include _partials/day3/critical_vs_atomic__reduction.qmd >}}

- Each thread gets a private copy of `sum`; the OpenMP runtime combines them at region exit. No serialisation in the hot loop.
- Cost ordering at high thread counts: `critical` ≫ `atomic` ≫ `reduction`.
- All three functions return the same number; reduction is the canonical idiom. Reach for `critical` / `atomic` only when a reduction can't express your accumulator.

## Why the memory model matters

The synchronisation primitives we just covered (`critical`, `atomic`, locks) work because OpenMP defines *when* writes become visible to other threads. Without a memory model, even this innocent-looking handshake is broken:

```cpp
// Thread A:                            // Thread B:
data = 42;                              while (!ready) ;
ready = true;                           printf("%d\n", data);
```

It can print **0**, **42**, or anything else — the compiler and CPU may reorder writes; the writes may sit in store buffers; cache coherence may not yet have propagated.

OpenMP 5.1 inherits the C++11 memory model and adds explicit `flush`, `acquire`, `release` semantics on top of the construct boundaries.

## `flush` — what it does

```cpp
shared_data = 42;
#pragma omp flush(shared_data)        // flush this variable
shared_ready = true;
#pragma omp flush                     // flush ALL accessible variables
```

- A `flush` is a *fence*: writes before it become visible after it; reads after it see writes-before.
- Without a list, all currently-accessible variables are flushed.
- 5.1 documents implicit-flush semantics for every other synchronisation primitive — most of the time you don't need explicit `flush`.

## Implicit flush points — when writes become visible

A thread's write becomes visible to other threads only at one of these synchronisation points; between them, the compiler is free to keep the value in a register or store buffer.

OpenMP 5.1 implicitly flushes at:

- Entry point & exit point of a `parallel` region.
- The barrier at the end of `for`, `single`, `sections` (unless `nowait`).
- Explicit `barrier`.
- `atomic` (with appropriate ordering clause).
- `critical` entry / exit.
- `omp_set_lock` / `omp_unset_lock`.
- `taskwait`, `taskgroup` end, `task` scheduling points.

For A2/A3, the implicit flushes at pragma boundaries usually suffice.

## Atomic memory ordering — the five clauses (5.1)

Every `atomic` directive takes an optional ordering clause. `seq_cst` is the default and the safe choice; relaxed orderings are cheaper on weakly-ordered CPUs (arm64, POWER) but only pay off when paired correctly.

- **`seq_cst`** *(default; legal on read / write / update / capture)* — every `seq_cst` access across all threads sits in a single global total order. Strongest, default, safe.
- **`release`** *(write / update / capture)* — prior reads and writes on this thread complete *before* this store. Pairs with an `acquire`-load on the consumer side — the *publishing* half of a handshake.
- **`acquire`** *(read / update / capture)* — subsequent reads and writes on this thread happen *after* this load. Pairs with a `release`-store on the producer side — the *subscribing* half.
- **`acq_rel`** *(update / capture)* — acquire on the read part + release on the write part of one read-modify-write. The right tool for compare-and-swap and lock-free queue updates.
- **`relaxed`** *(any kind)* — atomicity only, no ordering relative to other accesses. Use for standalone counters / statistics that don't gate any other state.

Fix for the `data` / `ready` race on the "Why the memory model matters" slide:

```cpp
// Thread A (publisher):              // Thread B (subscriber):
data = 42;                            int seen = 0;
#pragma omp atomic write release      do {
ready = 1;                            #pragma omp atomic read acquire
                                        seen = ready;
                                      } while (!seen);
                                      printf("%d\n", data);   // sees 42
```

The release-store synchronises-with the acquire-load: every memory effect *before* the release on A is observable *after* the acquire on B.

## Locks — when `critical` granularity isn't enough

`critical` ties granularity to *source code* — one global mutex per name.

When you need *data-driven* granularity (one mutex per data structure instance), use `omp_lock_t`:

- One lock per bucket of a hash map.
- One lock per node of a graph.
- One lock per row of a sparse matrix.

Independent operations against different locks proceed in parallel.

## `omp_lock_t` lifecycle

```cpp
omp_lock_t lk;
omp_init_lock(&lk);          // before use
// ...
omp_set_lock(&lk);           // acquire (blocks)
update_protected_data();
omp_unset_lock(&lk);         // release
// ...
omp_destroy_lock(&lk);       // before scope exit
```

- Plus a non-blocking `omp_test_lock(&lk)` returning bool.
- Plus `omp_nest_lock_t` for re-entrant cases (rare; avoid if you can).
- A RAII wrapper (a small C++ object whose destructor calls `omp_unset_lock` on scope exit) is recommended in production code.

## Locks — full example: per-bucket counter

{{< include _partials/day3/task_locks__bucketed_count.qmd >}}

- One `omp_lock_t` *per bucket*. Threads colliding on different buckets stay parallel — *data-driven* granularity, which `critical` (one mutex per source name) cannot express.
- Lifecycle in one place: `omp_init_lock` once at allocation, `set` / `unset` around every protected update, `omp_destroy_lock` once at teardown.
- `omp_lock_t` is not RAII — in production, wrap `set` / `unset` in a small guard so an exception inside the protected section can't leak the lock.
- Default to `atomic` or `reduction` first; reach for explicit locks only when per-record / per-node / per-bucket granularity matters.

## `single` — the canonical idiom

```cpp
#pragma omp parallel default(none) shared(setup)
{
#pragma omp single
    {
        do_one_shot_setup(setup);
    }
    // implicit barrier — every thread sees the result

    do_per_thread_work_using(setup);
}
```

- Exactly one (any) thread runs the block.
- Implicit barrier at exit — the rest of the team waits.
- Use for one-shot setup, lazy initialisation, or task-spawn loops.
- Add `nowait` if the rest of the team doesn't need to see what `single` did (e.g. one-thread logging) — but if they *do* read it, you've created a race.

## `masked` — `single` minus the barrier

```cpp
#pragma omp parallel
{
#pragma omp masked          // 5.1 (replaces deprecated `master`)
    {
        printf("from thread 0 only\n");
    }
    // NO barrier — other threads continue immediately

    do_per_thread_work();
}
```

- Only thread 0 (default) runs the block.
- *No* implicit barrier — other threads don't wait.
- 5.1's `masked` deprecates `master`. They're semantically equivalent; `masked` is more general (any thread can be designated).

## Why tasks?

`parallel for` distributes a known iteration space. But many problems aren't loops:

- Recursive divide-and-conquer (mergesort, quicksort, Barnes–Hut).
- Producer-consumer pipelines.
- Per-tile workloads with variable cost (Mandelbrot — A2).
- Graph traversal (e.g. breadth-first search).

A `task` is a unit of work the runtime queues + dispatches to threads. Tasks compose with parallel regions; tasks can spawn more tasks; the runtime balances load. (For loop-shaped problems inside a task region, `taskloop` is the worksharing-shaped cousin — covered later.)

## Task lifecycle

1. Encountered by a thread: `#pragma omp task { body }` packages `body` into a *task instance*.
2. Queued in the team's task pool.
3. Executed by *some* thread in the team — possibly the spawner itself, possibly another thread doing work-stealing.
4. Synchronisation point (`taskwait`, end of `taskgroup`, end of `parallel` region) ensures all child tasks finish.

Key: the spawning thread does **not** wait for the spawned task. It moves on.

## `task` example - post-order tree sum

{{< include _partials/day3/tasks_basic__tree_sum.qmd >}}

- Driver (in `tasks_basic.cpp`): a `parallel + single` block calls `tree_sum(root)` so exactly **one** thread seeds the recursion; the rest of the team pick up child tasks from the queue as the recursion fans out.
- Real-world relatives: quicksort, mergesort, Barnes–Hut tree walks, sparse Cholesky.

## `taskgroup` — synchronising the subtree

```cpp
#pragma omp taskgroup
{
#pragma omp task
    {
        deep_recursion_spawning_more_tasks();
    }
}
// at end of taskgroup: every descendant task has finished
```

- Stronger than `taskwait`: waits for every task spawned within the group, transitively.
- The right tool for "I want everything below this point to be done before I continue".

## Task data environment

```cpp
int outer = 42;
#pragma omp task
{ printf("%d\n", outer); }    // outer is firstprivate by default
```

- Variables captured from outer scope are **`firstprivate` by default** for tasks (unlike worksharing, where they're `shared`).
- The captured value is fixed at task creation, not at task execution. Subtle.
- To force shared, write `shared(outer)` explicitly. Common foot-gun.

## Task dependences — `out` → `in`

{{< include _partials/day3/task_dependences__chain.qmd >}}

- Tasks A and B form a chain: A produces `a`, then B consumes `a` and produces `b`. Source-code order is not what schedules the tasks — the runtime reads the `depend()` clauses on each task.

## Task dependences — what `depend()` means

| Clause | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `depend(out: x)` | "I write `x`" — must finish before any later `in: x` / `inout: x` on the same address. |
| `depend(in: x)` | "I read `x`" — wait for the most recent prior `out: x` / `inout: x`. |
| `depend(inout: x)` | Read-write — waits for prior writers, blocks subsequent readers. |

- The runtime builds a directed acyclic graph (DAG) — an ordering with no cycles — and schedules sibling tasks respecting that partial order.
- Address identity is what matters: two tasks listing **the same variable** in conflicting clauses are ordered; tasks on different variables are not.

## Task dependences — fan-in join

(Continuation: still inside the same `parallel` / `single` block.)

{{< include _partials/day3/task_dependences__join.qmd >}}

- Task D writes `d` independently of the A→B chain — runtime can run it in parallel with A and B.
- Task R declares `depend(in: b) depend(in: d)` — fan-in. The runtime waits for *both* upstream `out`s before scheduling R.
- Implicit barrier at the end of the `parallel` region waits for the whole DAG.
- Useful for irregular pipelines that don't fit a `taskloop`.

## Task DAG visualisation {visibility="uncounted"}

For `run_pipeline()` on the previous slide:

```
        out:a            in:a, out:b
        ┌──────┐         ┌──────┐
        │  A   │ ──────▶ │  B   │ ─┐
        └──────┘         └──────┘  │
        out:d                       ▼  in:b, in:d, out:r
        ┌──────┐                  ┌──────┐
        │  D   │ ────────────────▶│  R   │
        └──────┘                  └──────┘
```

- A and D are independent — runtime may run them in parallel.
- B waits for A; R waits for both B and D.
- The runtime infers all of this from `depend` clauses; you never name the dependences explicitly.

## `taskloop` — what it is

`taskloop` is a worksharing-shaped task generator: it splits an iteration space into ⌈n / grainsize⌉ *tasks* and dispatches them through the team's task pool.

{{< include _partials/day3/taskloop_apply__taskloop_form.qmd >}}

- Needs a surrounding parallel region; `single` ensures only one thread issues the `taskloop` (otherwise every team member generates the chunks). The combined `#pragma omp parallel masked taskloop` form collapses the boilerplate.
- Implicit `taskgroup` at the end — every chunk completes before the team continues. Use `nogroup` to strip it.

## `taskloop` vs `parallel for`

Same kernel, written with worksharing-for:

{{< include _partials/day3/taskloop_apply__for_form.qmd >}}

For a *flat, top-level* loop, `parallel for` (with whatever schedule fits the kernel — `static` here, `dynamic, C` for irregular) and `taskloop grainsize(C)` are roughly equivalent in performance. Reach for `taskloop` only when:

- You're already inside a `task` region — a nested `parallel for` would spin up a new team (typically a perf cliff; nested parallelism is often disabled by default).
- Chunks should *interleave* with sibling tasks on the same team.
- Chunks need to carry `depend(...)` or `priority(...)` (5.1).

Otherwise the `parallel for` form is simpler — no `single` boilerplate — and performs identically in practice.

## `task` vs `parallel for`

| Workload | Best fit |
|---|---|
| Regular loop, uniform per-iter cost | `parallel for` (`schedule(static)` or default) |
| Regular loop, irregular per-iter cost | `parallel for schedule(dynamic, C)` |
| Loop *inside* an enclosing task region | `taskloop grainsize(C)` (composes; no nested team) |
| Recursive / divide-and-conquer | `task` + `taskwait` (inside `single`) |
| Pipeline / dependence graph of independent tiles | `task` + `depend(...)` |
| Producer / consumer over a queue | `task` + `priority` clause (5.1) |

A2 lets you measure both forms side by side; expect roughly equivalent performance.

## Workload shape — uniform cost

`DAXPY` over a large vector:

```cpp
#pragma omp parallel for default(none) shared(a, b, c, n)
for (size_t i = 0; i < n; ++i)
    c[i] = a[i] + b[i];                // every iter the same cost
```

- `parallel for` with default `static` schedule: minimal overhead.
- `taskloop` would pay per-chunk scheduling overhead for no benefit.
- Use `taskloop` only when the loop is itself called from inside an enclosing task region or its chunks need to participate in a dependence graph — not as a generic "more advanced" loop construct.

::: {.callout-note}
DAXPY is a classic linear algebra operation whose name comes from:

Double-precision A·X Plus Y
:::

## Workload shape — irregular cost

Mandelbrot escape-time test:

```cpp
for (auto& tile : tiles) {
    for (auto pixel : tile) {
        // 1 to 1000 iters depending on c position
        compute_escape(pixel);
    }
}
```

- Per-tile cost ratio can exceed 100×.
- `parallel for static` puts the expensive tiles on whichever thread happens to own that range — easy 2× imbalance.
- `parallel for schedule(dynamic, C)` work-steals to balance — the natural fit for a flat outer loop.

## `grainsize` — too small vs too big

| Symptom | Likely cause |
|---|---|
| At low thread counts, taskloop runs slower than serial | grainsize too small — task dispatch dominates |
| At high thread counts, some threads idle while one finishes | grainsize too big — last task's tail dominates |
| Speedup plateaus around 4-8× regardless of P | task pool contention (rare; tune queueing strategy) |

For A2: tile size 100 × 100 ⇒ ~1250 tiles ⇒ grainsize 8 = ~150 tasks. Reasonable starting point.

## A2 brief — what you'll be doing

**Kernel**: count Mandelbrot-set membership over a 5000 × 5000 grid (post-symmetry: 5000 × 2500 sampled, doubled — see `mandelbrot.h`).

You implement **two** variants:

- `mandelbrot_for.cpp` — `#pragma omp parallel for collapse(2)` over the upper-half tile grid.
- `mandelbrot_tasks.cpp` — `#pragma omp taskloop grainsize(...)` over the same tile grid.

Plus one production recommendation in `CHOICE.md`. The image region has irregular per-tile cost but isn't pathological — neither variant is rigged to win.

## CHOICE.md format and grading

```
---
recommended: tasks                        # or: parallel_for
measured_for_s: 0.49
measured_tasks_s: 0.31
justification_keyword: irregular_load_balance   # only if you recommend the slower variant
---
```

The grader parses this header. Your recommendation passes if it matches the variant your **own** committed `perf-results-a2.json` shows as faster, OR if you recommend the slower one with a defensible keyword. **Either variant can be the right answer**.

The canonical re-run is *not consulted* for CHOICE.

## A2 deliverables checklist

| Path | What |
|---|---|
| `assignment-2/mandelbrot_for.cpp` | parallel-for variant |
| `assignment-2/mandelbrot_tasks.cpp` | tasks variant |
| `assignment-2/CHOICE.md` | structured header + ≤ 200 word justification |
| `assignment-2/answers.csv` | 15 MCQ answers |
| `assignment-2/tables.csv` | per-variant per-thread-count times |
| `assignment-2/REFLECTION.md` | required headers, ≥ 50 words per section |
| `assignment-2/perf-results-a2.json` | hyperfine output from your CX3 self-bench |
